name: Ratnakīrti
dates_notes: Conventionally dated c. 1000–1050 CE on the basis of his being Jñānaśrīmitra's direct disciple (Jñānaśrīmitra c. 980–1040) and of his treatises being explicitly cited by Udayana (c. 1050) in the *Ātma-Tattva-Viveka* and *Nyāya-Kusumāñjali*. The Thakur (1957, *Ratnakīrti-Nibandhāvalī*) edition is the standard reference; Mimaki 1976 and McCrea–Patil 2010 confirm the window.

lineage_polemical.note: Udayana's *Ātma-Tattva-Viveka* (the Discernment of the Truth of the Self) is the principal Navya-Nyāya refutation of the Buddhist no-self position; Ratnakīrti's *Sthira-Siddhi-Dūṣaṇa* (Refutation of the Establishment of Permanence) and *Kṣaṇa-Bhaṅga-Siddhi* (Establishment of Momentariness) are the targeted Buddhist positions. Udayana names Ratnakīrti explicitly; the exchange is the canonical Buddhist-Nyāya debate on selfhood and momentariness.

core_thesis: Ratnakīrti is Jñānaśrīmitra's principal disciple and the most aggressive late Buddhist pramāṇavādin. His treatises (collected in the *Ratnakīrti-Nibandhāvalī*, eleven short prose works) deploy the Dharmakīrtian inferential apparatus polemically against the principal Brahmanical positions of his generation. The *Kṣaṇa-Bhaṅga-Siddhi* (Establishment of Momentariness) is the canonical late-period defense of the Buddhist doctrine of universal momentariness via the *sattvānumāna* (the inference from existence to momentariness): whatever exists must be causally efficacious, and only the momentary can be causally efficacious, therefore whatever exists is momentary. The *Sthira-Siddhi-Dūṣaṇa* (Refutation of the Establishment of Permanence) attacks the Naiyāyika and Mīmāṃsaka arguments for any non-momentary entity. The *Apoha-Siddhi* (Establishment of Exclusion) refines Jñānaśrīmitra's apoha-theory of universals. The *Pratibandha-Siddhi* (Establishment of Pervasion) supplies the Buddhist account of the universal-relation grounding inference. The *Citrādvaita-Prakāśavāda* (Manifestation of Variegated Non-Duality) is doctrinally distinctive: it articulates a Yogācāra position on which a single conscious moment can hold structurally variegated content (the variegated *citra*-image) without the variegation requiring multiple distinct cognitive events — a position with significant implications for the apoha-theory and for the *sākāra*/*nirākāra* debate. The *Santānāntara-Dūṣaṇa* (Refutation of Other Mind-streams) raises the late Yogācāra problem of solipsism: whether the postulation of other mind-streams is consistent with vijñapti-mātratā. Ratnakīrti's position is that on the *paramārtha* (ultimate) level the postulation cannot be sustained, while at the *vyavahāra* (conventional) level it is presupposed by the bodhisattva-vow. The *Īśvara-Sādhana-Dūṣaṇa* refutes the Naiyāyika theistic arguments.

engaged_works.title: Ratnakirti-Nibandhavali
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Eleven short prose treatises securely attributed to Ratnakīrti; the collected Sanskrit text edited by Thakur (1957, revised 1975). Most treatises survive only in this single edited corpus from a Tibetan-preserved manuscript.

ascription_evidence.description: Thakur 1957/1975 critical edition; Mimaki 1976 La Réfutation Bouddhique de la Permanence des Choses; McCrea and Patil 2010 study of the apoha-treatises.

engaged_works.summary: Eleven self-standing prose treatises covering the principal disputed questions of eleventh-century Buddhist-Hindu debate. The *Kṣaṇa-Bhaṅga-Siddhi* defends universal momentariness via the *sattvānumāna*. The *Sthira-Siddhi-Dūṣaṇa* refutes the establishment of permanence. The *Apoha-Siddhi* refines the apoha-theory of universals. The *Pratibandha-Siddhi* supplies the analysis of *vyāpti* (pervasion) grounding valid inference. The *Citrādvaita-Prakāśavāda* defends the doctrine of variegated non-duality within a single conscious moment. The *Santānāntara-Dūṣaṇa* refutes the establishment of other mind-streams. The *Sarvajña-Siddhi* establishes the omniscience of the Buddha. The *Īśvara-Sādhana-Dūṣaṇa* refutes Naiyāyika theistic arguments. The *Vyāpti-Nirṇaya* refines the pervasion-analysis. The *Anupalabdhi-Rahasya* treats non-perception as a pramāṇa. The *Pramāṇāntar-Bhāva-Prakaraṇa* treats the question of additional pramāṇas. The treatises are short and polemically focused; together they constitute the most concentrated surviving witness of late Indian Buddhist analytic philosophy.

reception_notes: Ratnakīrti is the last major systematic Buddhist philosopher to be cited extensively by name in the Brahmanical scholastic literature. Udayana's *Ātma-Tattva-Viveka* is structured as a refutation of Ratnakīrti's no-self and momentariness positions; Gangeśa's *Tattvacintāmaṇi* engages Ratnakīrti's apoha-theory in the *Apohavāda* section. After Ratnakīrti the Buddhist scholastic tradition continues at Vikramaśīla for another century or so but produces no comparably influential original works. The Sanskrit Vedāntic literature does not engage Ratnakīrti directly by name; the engagement is with the Buddhist positions in their generic form.
